event-brief:quebec-settlement:brief.spoken: In 1534–1535 Jacques Cartier sailed up the St. Lawrence, visited Stadacona (today's Québec City), and claimed the valley for France as "Canada." France's early colonization was disrupted by religious wars at home until the late 1500s. For our tree, this French doorway leads to the Mary Ann Goodwater branch.

event-brief:quebec-settlement:standard.spoken: In 1534, France's King Francis I authorized navigator Jacques Cartier, a French-Breton maritime explorer, to lead a voyage to the New World to seek gold and other riches, as well as a new sea route to Asia. In 1535, Jacques Cartier and his crew sailed up the St. Lawrence River and visited an Iroquois settlement of about 500 persons called "Stadacona" on the bluff where Québec City stands today. Cartier named the river the St. Lawrence. While in the New World he claimed the large St. Lawrence River valley for France, calling the area "Canada", after the Huron-Iroquois word "kanata". Cartier's later expeditions up the St. Lawrence and the building of Fort Charlesbourg-Royal enabled France to lay claim to the lands that would become the French colony of Québec. Between 1562 and 1598, France's colonization efforts in North America were disrupted by religious wars at home between French Roman Catholics and the rising French Protestant Huguenots. In 1578, eighty-six years after Columbus reached the Americas, England began its own efforts to explore and settle the New World. The English monarchy and early Parliament had been distracted by wars in Scotland, Ireland, and France, and by Protestant–Catholic conflict at home — leaving little energy for the New World until later in the 1600s. For the Winifred Coss Family Tree, this is the doorway to the Goodwater branch: French settlers and farmers whose descendants eventually meet the Powers branch in Iowa in 1853.

event-brief:quebec-settlement:deep.spoken: In 1534, France's King Francis I authorized navigator Jacques Cartier to explore the New World. Cartier's 1535 voyage up the St. Lawrence brought him to Stadacona — an Iroquois settlement on the bluff where Québec City stands — and he claimed the river valley for France, naming the country "Canada" from the Huron-Iroquois word "kanata." Fort Charlesbourg-Royal and later expeditions let France hold its claim to what became the colony of Québec. Meanwhile, between 1562 and 1598, France's colonization was repeatedly interrupted by wars between Roman Catholics and Huguenot Protestants at home — the same era of religious upheaval that shapes the English chapters of this story. England's own sustained New World push came later: by 1578 the crown was still absorbed in Scotland, Ireland, France, and domestic Protestant–Catholic conflict. The two colonial stories run in parallel for generations before they meet in one family. For the Winifred Coss Family Tree, Québec is not background colour — it is the origin of the Mary Ann Goodwater branch. French farmers and settlers along the St. Lawrence are direct ancestors on that line. Their descendants carry the name Goodwater forward until, in 1853, Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Ann Goodwater and the Goodwater and Powers branches join.

event-brief:salem-witch-trials:brief.spoken: In 1691, Martha (Barrett) Sparks of Chelmsford was accused of witchcraft and sent to a Boston prison on 28 October, while her husband Henry Sparks was away fighting in King Philip's War. She is a direct family tie on our Powers branch through the Barrett and Sparks lines in Massachusetts.

event-brief:salem-witch-trials:standard.spoken: In 1691 the Province of Massachusetts became famous for its witch trials. The panic did not begin in Salem itself: it started in Chelmsford, about thirty miles west of Salem, when Martha (Barrett) Sparks was accused of witchcraft. Martha was married to Henry Sparks, who was away fighting in King Philip's War when she was accused and imprisoned on suspicion of witchcraft. On 28 October 1691 she was sent to a Boston prison. Our family is not merely living in the same era — Martha is named in the family story as an accused prisoner. The Barrett name continues in this chapter through Rebecca Barrett, who marries Walter Powers III; their son Josiah Powers Sr. is born in 1710 on Neshoba Hill in Littleton. That Powers line is the Edith Powers branch of the Winifred Coss Family Tree. Dad's document records the accusation and imprisonment but does not state the final outcome of Martha's case.

event-brief:salem-witch-trials:deep.spoken: In 1691 the Province of Massachusetts became famous for its witch trials. The hysteria began in Chelmsford — thirty miles west of Salem — when Martha (Barrett) Sparks was accused of witchcraft. On 28 October 1691 she was sent to a Boston prison. Her husband Henry Sparks was away fighting in King Philip's War when she was accused. This is a direct family involvement, not just "ancestors living nearby." Martha (Barrett) Sparks is named in the timeline and story as an accused prisoner. Her Barrett kin had settled in Massachusetts generations earlier: Thomas Barrett and Margaret (Huntington) moved from Braintree to Chelmsford in 1663; their son Joseph Barrett married Martha Gould in 1672; Rebecca Barrett was born to that couple in 1673. On 1 November 1692, Martha Sparks's father Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned the Governor and Council for her release (Massachusetts Archives, Vol. 135 No. 64). The story chapter also notes Thomas Barrett Jr. on 6 December 1692, and points readers to the "Story of Jessie Maud Paff branch of Morris Rankin Batie's Family Tree" for more on the wider witch-trials history. The Powers branch meets the Barrett line when Walter Powers III and Rebecca (Barrett) marry; their son Josiah Powers Sr. is born 22 September 1710 in a Garrison House on Neshoba Hill, Littleton. That Josiah line carries forward to Edith Powers and eventually to the 1853 marriage of Joseph Warren Coss and Mary Ann Goodwater. What the family sources do not record: whether Martha was ever tried, acquitted, or executed. Dad's document states the accusation, imprisonment, and her father's petition — and leaves the final outcome unstated.

search:event-evt-1613-140:brief.spoken: Note: From 1613 to 1620, the “Compagnie des Marchands“ was a monopoly Note: From 1613 to 1620, the “Compagnie des Marchands“ was a monopoly company supported by the French government that operated in Canada but failed to fulfill their contractual obligations and therefore lost their monopoly rights in 1621 to the “ Compagnie de Montmorency”.

search:chapter-beaver-wars:standard.spoken: Lawrence River Valley and the lower Great Lakes region of North America. The Iroquois Confederation, was led by the Mohawk tribe. On 28 Jan 1648, Etienne Dumay Sr. was working for Robert Giffard at the time of the marriage. On 4 Feb 1648, Anne (Cloutier) died in the Québec hospital.

search:chapter-beaver-wars:deep.spoken: Lawrence River Valley and the lower Great Lakes region of North America. The Iroquois Confederation, was led by the Mohawk tribe. On 28 Jan 1648, Etienne Dumay Sr. was working for Robert Giffard at the time of the marriage. On 4 Feb 1648, Anne (Cloutier) died in the Québec hospital. Robert and Marie were married and in their marriage contract Marie made sure that Robert agreed to find a house in Québec City for the family’s new home. In 1648, Catherine Rivet was born to Pierre Rivet and Matie (Sorgeaut) in Étampes, Île-de-France, France. In 1648, Antoine Dubois was born to Pierre Dubois and Philippe (Guillionne) in Varaize, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France. In 1648, Mathurine Goard was born to Gilles Goard and Catharine (Leger) in Saint-Sulpice, Occitania, France. On 1 Mar 1649, Anne Le Seigneur was born to Guillaume Seigneur and Jeanne Madeliene (Sauve) in Rouen, Normandy, France.

search:chapter-life-in-the-canada-colony.spoken: Men could rise by their own hard work and efforts or come down through their own mistakes and lack of effort. Everyone’s place in the colony was not determined for them by the accident of their birth parents.

Men could rise by their own hard work and efforts or come down through their own mistakes and lack of effort. Everyone’s place in the colony was not determined for them by the accident of their birth parents. In 1620, French King Louis XIII ordered Samuel de Champlain to cease exploration and to return to Québec , and to devoted himself to the administration of the Canada. Lawrence River valley until his death in 1635. In 1621, Robert Giffard, a gentleman-surgeon and apothecary, made his first trip to the Quebec settlement Canada.

search:chapter-pequot-war:brief.spoken: Between Jul 1636 and Sep 1638, the “Pequot War” occurred when various Native American tribes fought for control of beaver fur pelts trading business in the New England area. search:event-evt-1629-238:brief.spoken: On 9 Aug 1629, Samuel de Champlain formally surrendered his beloved Q On 9 Aug 1629, Samuel de Champlain formally surrendered his beloved Québec settlement to the English Kirke brothers. search:event-evt-1633-271:brief.spoken: On 22 Mar 1633, Samuel de Champlain returned to the Québec settlement On 22 Mar 1633, Samuel de Champlain returned to the Québec settlement and began construction of a new settlement based on the framework of a traditional French “ville” (town) that was common in France at the time. The Québec settlement’s population was split into two groups.

On 12 Oct 1665, Nicolas Massard and Anne Bellesoeur were married in Québec City. On 17 Nov 1665, Sébastien Gingras and Marie Genevieve Guillebourg were married in Sillery, Canada. In 1665, eighteen-year-old Jacques Samson, left Normandy, France with his older brother Gabriel and sailed to Canada. On 7 Jan 1666, Pierre Duchesne and Catherine Rivet were married in Montmorency, Canada. In 1666, eighteen-year-old Mathurine Goard arrived in Ville Marie settlement as one of the “filles du Roi” women. On 26 Apr 1666, Charles Maheu was born to Pierre Maheu and Jeanne (Drouin) in Chateau Richer, Canada. On 6 Sep 1666, Laurent Migneron and Anne Saint-Denis were married in Chateau-Richer, Canada.

search:chapter-filles-marier-or-marriage.spoken: Between 1634 and 1663, 262 filles à marier or “Marriageable Girls” immigrated to Canada representing one quarter of all the single girls arriving in Canada through 1673. They were promised nothing but the possibility of a better life.

Between 1634 and 1663, 262 filles à marier or “Marriageable Girls” immigrated to Canada representing one quarter of all the single girls arriving in Canada through 1673. They were promised nothing but the possibility of a better life. In 1634, Noël Langlois Sr. left France and sailed to the Quebec settlement as part of the Robert Giffard’s “Percheron Immigration” to the Quebec settlement in Canada. On 1 Oct 1634, after arriving back in the Québec settlement, Hélène Desportes married Guillaume Hébert, who had stayed in the Québec settlement when it was under English control.

Between 1634 and 1663, 262 filles à marier or “Marriageable Girls” immigrated to Canada representing one quarter of all the single girls arriving in Canada through 1673. search:event-evt-1636-308:brief.spoken: In Aug 1636, Jean Côté was granted a lot on the Grande-Allee, a boule In Aug 1636, Jean Côté was granted a lot on the Grande-Allee, a boulevard, which today is considered the “Champs Elysees” of Quebec City by the new Governor Montmagny of Quebec. (Champs Elysees is the main street in Paris France that runs under the Arc de Triomphe).

search:event-evt-1636-303:brief.spoken: Note: Private initiative and capital had always been involved in the Note: Private initiative and capital had always been involved in the operations of the British colonies, and the colonists were expected to provide for their own defense. By 1636, ten “Train Bands” (volunteer military companies) were formed in Boston and nearby towns.

search:event-evt-1637-318:brief.spoken: In 1637, Nicholas Holt was one of ten men from the town of Newbury wh In 1637, Nicholas Holt was one of ten men from the town of Newbury who traveled 40 miles on foot to qualify for the “freeman oath” so they could vote for John Winthrop in the election for Governor of the Massachusetts Bay Colony to prevent Sir Henry Vanc from being re-elected.

In 1661, the Île d’Orléans, a large island in the middle of the St. Sainte-Famille, the first parish on the Île d’Orléans Island, was established in 1661 after a horrible attack. Four months later, widow Euphrosine Leblanc (Nicolet) married Elie Dussault in the Québec set… On 27 Aug 1662, Marie-Françoise Gaudry was born to Nicolas Gaudry and Agnés (Morin) in the Québec settlement in Canada. On 24 Oct 1662, Marie Anne Lemarché was born to Jean Lemarché and Catherine (Hurault) in Vieux, the Québec settlement i… In 1662, Denise Pilote (Gauthier), wife of Léonard Pilote, arrived from France with their nine-year-old daughter Marguerite and six-year-old son Jean Pilote in the Québec settlement in Canada. In 1662, Pierre Duchesne sailed from Picardie France to the Québec settlement. Pierre worked as a “servant” for three years to fulfill his contract for his passage to Canada before becoming a colonist in Canada. In 1662, Canada’s new Governor Pierre Dubois Davaugour, gave Étienne Dumay Sr. The Dumay family name later changed to Demers. On 13 May 1663, Jean Guyon died in the middle of the series of earth quakes that shook the Beaupré settlement.

search:event-evt-1670-586:brief.spoken: On 2 May 1670, the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) was chartered making On 2 May 1670, the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) was chartered making it the oldest incorporated joint-stock merchandising company in the English-speaking world. HBC was a fur trading business that was entwined with the colonization of North America, particularly in Canada.

search:chapter-king-philip-s-war-the-pro.spoken: In 1675, Native-Americans in New England made their last major effort to drive the English colonists out of their ancestorial territory in the “King Philip’s War”. The war was named for King Philip, the Wampanoag Chief who adopted the Engl… In Sep 1675, Phineas Upham received a commission as a Lieutenant (Lt.) serving under Captain Wayte in the “King Philip’s War”.

In 1675, Native-Americans in New England made their last major effort to drive the English colonists out of their ancestorial territory in the “King Philip’s War”. The war was named for King Philip, the Wampanoag Chief who adopted the Engl… In Sep 1675, Phineas Upham received a commission as a Lieutenant (Lt.) serving under Captain Wayte in the “King Philip’s War”. On 2 Nov 1675, “King Philip’s War” was formally declared against the Narragansetts Native American tribe. In 1675, John Preston served as a solder under Captain Gardner in the “King Philip’s War”. In 1675, during the “King Philip’s War” fighting, William Ward’s house was burned down, his cattle were killed and one of his son’s was killed.

In 1675, Native-Americans in New England made their last major effort to drive the English colonists out of their ancestorial territory in the “King Philip’s War”. The war was named for King Philip, the Wampanoag Chief who adopted the Engl… In Sep 1675, Phineas Upham received a commission as a Lieutenant (Lt.) serving under Captain Wayte in the “King Philip’s War”. On 2 Nov 1675, “King Philip’s War” was formally declared against the Narragansetts Native American tribe. In 1675, John Preston served as a solder under Captain Gardner in the “King Philip’s War”. In 1675, during the “King Philip’s War” fighting, William Ward’s house was burned down, his cattle were killed and one of his son’s was killed. On 16 Jul 1677, at the end of the “King Philip’s War”, widower William Taylor married again widow Hannah (Merriam), the widow of Henry Axtell, who had been killed by Native warriors on the Marlborough-Sudbury Road in April, 1676. Hannah ha… On 28 Jan 1678, forty-seven-year-old Nathaniel Gary died from smallpox in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. Widow Anne Mattle (Douglas) remarried five-years later in 1683 a Thomas Bishop in the town of Roxbury. On 11 Apr 1678, Eliezer Taylor was born to William Taylor and Hannah (Merriam). On 8 Jun 1679, Dorothy Lamb was born to Joshua Lamb and Mary (Alcock) in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

search:event-evt-1682-645:brief.spoken: The town of Roxbury in the Connecticut Colony had been an Indian Pray The town of Roxbury in the Connecticut Colony had been an Indian Praying village called Wabaquasset that got involved in the “King Philip’s War”. The Native people on the north side of the village had sided with King Philip and join in the fight against the English colonists.

search:chapter-king-william-s-war:brief.spoken: In 1689, the “King William’s War”, started in North America between France and England. A year later in Oct 1690, the “Battle of Québec” was fought between Canada and British Massachusetts Bay Colony’s militia.

search:chapter-king-william-s-war:standa.spoken: In 1689, the “King William’s War”, started in North America between France and England. A year later in Oct 1690, the “Battle of Québec” was fought between Canada and British Massachusetts Bay Colony’s militia. Note: For the British/American Colonies full prospective on the four “French and Indian wars” see the Edith Powers’ Family Tree Story back at the beginning of this document. In 1689, during an Iroquois raid on a large Miami Native settleme… On 29 Mar 1690, Pierre Charles Le Sueur and Marguerite Messier were married in Boucherville, Canada. On 18 Oct 1691, Dominique Bonneau Sr. was born to Joseph Bonneau and Madeleine (Duchesne) in Montmorency, Canada. On 18 Feb 1692, Jean Jacoti and Anne Marie Massard were married in Québec City, Canada. (Jean Jacoti must have arrived in Canada before this marriage, but no record of his arrival has been found.) In 1692 Toussaint Giroux spent a month in the Québec hospital for unknown reasons. In 1711, he sold a tract of his land to his grandson Raphaël on the condition he care for him in his old age and pay a sum of money to each of his children… On 1 Oct 1692, Guillaume Labelle was hospitalized at the Hotel-Dieu of Québec City for 40 days.

search:event-evt-1691-685:brief.spoken: In 1691, the Province of Massachusetts became famous for its “witch t In 1691, the Province of Massachusetts became famous for its “witch trials”. The witch hysteria started in the town of Chelmsford, which was 30 miles west of Salem, when Martha (Barrett) Sparks was accused of witchcraft and on 28 Oct 1691 was sent to a Boston prison.

search:event-evt-1803-838:brief.spoken: In 1803, the “Louisiana Purchase” occurred when the United States pur In 1803, the “Louisiana Purchase” occurred when the United States purchased France’s interest in North America for $15 million dollars. search:event-evt-1846-880:standard.spoken: Palmyra in Jefferson Co. Wisconsin was first settled 1846 by Cyrus No Palmyra in Jefferson Co. Wisconsin was first settled 1846 by Cyrus Norton. Three years later a saw mill was built by the David Johnson Powers and his brothers who named the new settlement “Palmyra” after a city in the Syrian desert, because of the abundance of sand in the area.

search:event-evt-1854-889:brief.spoken: In 1854, the old French feudal seigneurial system in Québec Lower Can In 1854, the old French feudal seigneurial system in Québec Lower Canada was finally officially abolished, after Benjamin Goodwater III and Mary Louise (Carriere) and their children had left Québec seeking land and opportunity in the United States.
